_meta.description: Comprehensive Klipper error reference — every common MCU, TMC/motor driver, thermal, motion, and CAN error with the exact mechanism that raises it in the Klipper source, diagnostics, and fixes. Used by klipper_error_lookup.py and ingested into the Graphify knowledge graph.
_meta.source_of_truth: Klipper source (klippy/*.py, klippy/extras/*.py, src/*.c) — use klipper_source_manager.py --locate-error to see the exact raise site

mcu_communication.description: Errors in the host↔MCU serial/USB/CAN link — klippy/mcu.py, klippy/serialhdl.py, klippy/serialqueue

errors.why: The host opened (or failed to open) the serial device but never completed the identify handshake — the MCU did not answer the 'identify' command with its data dictionary. The MCU is absent, not running Klipper firmware, running a different firmware version/dictionary, or the wrong device node was configured.
common_causes: Wrong or stale 'serial:' path in [mcu] — device re-enumerated (use /dev/serial/by-id/)
MCU never flashed, or flashed with mismatched Klipper version after a host update
Board held in DFU/boot mode, or crashed before USB enumeration
USB cable is power-only (no data lines) or damaged
Baud mismatch on UART-connected MCUs
diagnostics: ls /dev/serial/by-id/ — is the board enumerated at all?
dmesg | tail — USB connect/disconnect or enumeration errors
After host update: reflash MCU (version must match klippy)
fixes: Point [mcu] serial: at the current /dev/serial/by-id/ path
Reflash Klipper firmware matching the host version (make flash)
Replace USB cable with a known-good data cable

errors.why: The serial transport died mid-session: the low-level serialqueue stopped receiving acknowledgements and retransmits were exhausted, or the OS closed the device node. This is a transport-layer failure, not a firmware logic error — the MCU either reset (watchdog/brownout), lost USB (EMI, cable), or the CAN bridge dropped.
common_causes: Undervoltage/brownout resetting the MCU or the Pi's USB controller (check vcgencmd get_throttled)
EMI from stepper/heater wiring coupling into an unshielded USB cable
Loose USB connector vibrating during fast moves
5V rail sag when heaters/fans switch
CAN bus errors reaching bus-off state (for CAN-bridge MCUs)
diagnostics: pi_system_diagnostics.py --check power — decode throttling flags first
dmesg — 'usb disconnect' timestamps vs klippy.log shutdown time
ip -details -s link show can0 — error counters/bus-off for CAN setups
klipper_log_parser.py — look at 'Stats' lines: bytes_retransmit rising before the drop
fixes: Fix power delivery (proper 5V/3A+ supply, short thick cables)
Shielded/shorter USB cable routed away from motor and heater wiring
For CAN: verify both 120Ω terminators and bitrate 500000 on all nodes

errors.example: Unable to open serial port: [Errno 2] could not open port
errors.why: The OS refused to open the configured device node — it does not exist, or permissions block the klipper user. Klipper never even reached the MCU.
common_causes: Device path typo or board unplugged/re-enumerated to a different node
klipper user not in dialout/tty group
Another process (OctoPrint serial connection, debug terminal) holds the port
diagnostics: ls -la /dev/serial/by-id/
lsof /dev/ttyACM0 (or the configured node) — who holds it?
groups klipper-user
fixes: Use the stable /dev/serial/by-id/ path
In OctoPrint, keep the serial connection set to VIRTUAL/Klipper, never the raw MCU port
usermod -aG dialout <user> and re-login

errors.why: The MCU's compiled command dictionary differs from what the host expects — host and firmware were built from different Klipper commits. Klipper's host↔MCU protocol is regenerated per build, so versions must match exactly.
common_causes: Host updated (git pull / update manager) without reflashing MCUs
One of several MCUs (toolhead board!) reflashed but not the others
diagnostics: Compare 'Klipper version' in klippy.log against firmware version in the connect banner for EVERY [mcu]
fixes: Rebuild and reflash ALL MCUs (mainboard + toolhead + Pi mcu if used) after every Klipper update

errors.why: The periodic clock synchronization query (host polls each MCU's clock to map MCU ticks↔host time) went unanswered. The link is saturated, glitching, or the MCU stalled. Clock sync failures precede most 'Timer too close' cascades on marginal links.
common_causes: Same transport causes as Lost communication
Severe host CPU starvation (SD card I/O stalls, throttling)
diagnostics: visualize_data.py --type stats — srtt/rttvar growth
pi_system_diagnostics.py --check storage
fixes: Fix the transport/power issue; move host off failing SD card

mcu_shutdown.description: Firmware-initiated shutdowns — string literals in src/*.c, surfaced by klippy as 'MCU shutdown: <reason>'

errors.raised_in: src/sched.c (sched_timer_dispatch: waketime already in the past)
errors.why: The MCU scheduler dequeued a timer whose wake time had ALREADY passed — the MCU could not keep up with the event stream. Every step pulse, PWM edge, and ADC sample is a hard-deadline timer; when per-tick load exceeds what the MCU core can service (or the host delivered a command burst late), the deadline is missed and the firmware shuts down rather than lose motion integrity.
common_causes: Too many step events: very high microstepping (256) + high speed on multiple steppers
Overloaded MCU also driving SPI displays/neopixels with tight timing
Host-side stall delivered a late burst (SD card pause, CPU throttle) — the MCU then sees past-due timers
CAN bus congestion delaying toolhead MCU commands
A single underpowered MCU (8-bit AVR) asked for modern speeds
diagnostics: klipper_log_parser.py — check Stats: mcu_task_avg/mcu_task_stddev climbing before shutdown
Which MCU shut down? (mainboard vs toolhead) — points at USB vs CAN path
pi_system_diagnostics.py --check power/thermal — host stall causes
fixes: Reduce microsteps (16 is plenty with interpolation), lower max_velocity/accel
Move ancillary devices (displays, LEDs) off the motion MCU
Fix host stalls (SD card, throttling); for CAN raise txqueuelen, check bus load

errors.example: MCU shutdown: Missed scheduling of next digital out event
errors.why: Same deadline-miss family as 'Timer too close' but for a specific queued event type (digital pin, hard PWM): the scheduled toggle time for a pin event passed before the MCU serviced it. For heater/fan pins Klipper treats this as safety-critical and shuts down.
common_causes: MCU overload (see timer_too_close)
Extremely small cycle_time on a soft PWM pin
Host comm stall
diagnostics: Identify the pin/event in the shutdown message
Same stats analysis as timer_too_close
fixes: Increase cycle_time on PWM pins (0.010 default heaters)
Reduce MCU load; use hardware PWM pins where possible

errors.why: The firmware main loop stopped petting the hardware watchdog for the timeout period — the MCU core was hung or stuck in a fault handler. This is a hardware/electrical-level fault, not scheduling pressure: crystal dropout, silicon fault, severe EMI, or supply glitch froze the core.
common_causes: Power supply glitch/brownout on the MCU rail
EMI strike (static discharge through probe/endstop wiring)
Failing crystal/board fault
diagnostics: Correlate with heater/motor switching events
Inspect grounding; check if repeatable at a specific print moment
fixes: Fix supply and grounding; replace board if it recurs without electrical cause

errors.raised_in: src/command.c (host sent explicit shutdown), triggered from klippy
errors.why: Not a fault in itself — the HOST commanded this MCU to shut down, almost always because ANOTHER MCU or host module shut down first and klippy propagated emergency-stop to all MCUs. The real error is the FIRST shutdown line in klippy.log (or an M112).
common_causes: Cascade from the real failing MCU (e.g. toolhead CAN board)
User/macro issued M112
diagnostics: klipper_log_parser.py — find the FIRST shutdown event before the cascade
fixes: Diagnose the original shutdown; this one is a symptom

errors.example: MCU 'mcu' shutdown: ADC out of range
errors.why: A thermistor ADC sample fell outside the sanity window the host computed from min_temp/max_temp for that sensor. An open circuit reads full-scale (≈max ADC → reports very low temp), a short reads ≈0. The firmware cannot distinguish 'broken sensor' from 'on fire', so it shuts down. It is a HARDWARE/wiring signal in ~90% of cases — or the configured sensor_type/pullup doesn't match the physical sensor.
common_causes: Thermistor wire fatigue/break at the hotend (open) — most common
Crimped/pinched thermistor lead shorting to ground
Loose Molex/JST connector on toolhead or mainboard
Wrong sensor_type (e.g. Generic 3950 configured for EPCOS 100K B57560G104F — Fracktal standard)
min_temp: 0 in an unheated chamber that dips below 0°C at startup
diagnostics: Measure thermistor resistance at connector (EPCOS 100K ≈100kΩ @25°C)
Wiggle-test the harness while watching temps (visualize_data.py --type temperature)
Compare which sensor: extruder vs bed vs chamber narrows the harness
fixes: Repair/replace thermistor or harness segment
Correct sensor_type in the toolhead cfg
Widen min_temp/max_temp only if legitimately out of range (cold room)

errors.why: The host streamed more queued moves than the MCU's RAM-limited move queue can hold — host flow-control accounting and firmware queue size disagree. Rare in normal operation; indicates a host bug, a firmware/host version mismatch, or custom firmware with a shrunken queue.
common_causes: Host/firmware version mismatch
Out-of-tree firmware build with reduced RAM
diagnostics: Reflash matching firmware; check for custom builds
fixes: Reflash all MCUs from the same source tree as the host

errors.example: MCU shutdown: Stepper 'stepper_x' too far in past
errors.why: A step event's scheduled time was already in the past when the stepper driver code loaded it — the step queue ran dry then received stale events. Effectively 'Timer too close' localized to one stepper's step compression stream, same root causes.
common_causes: See timer_too_close
CAN latency for toolhead-driven steppers
diagnostics: Which stepper? maps to which MCU/bus
Stats analysis as timer_too_close
fixes: Same as timer_too_close

tmc_drivers.description: Trinamic driver errors — klippy/extras/tmc.py decodes GSTAT/DRV_STATUS and raises with flag annotations. SPI (TMC5160/2130) and UART (TMC2209/2208) variants. Fracktal: TMC5160 X/Y/Z (SPI, 1.2A), TMC2209 extruder (UART, 0.85A).

errors.example: Unable to read tmc uart 'stepper_e' register IFCNT
errors.why: The single-wire UART transaction to a TMC2209/2208 got no valid reply. The driver is unpowered, the PDN_UART wiring/pin assignment is wrong, the uart_address doesn't match the driver's MS1/MS2 strapping, or two drivers collide on one address.
common_causes: VM (motor voltage) absent — TMC logic won't respond on UART without VM on many boards
Wrong uart_pin / tx_pin in config
uart_address mismatch or duplicate on shared UART
Driver socket not fully seated (plug-in driver boards)
diagnostics: Did it EVER work? If new build → wiring/address; if sudden → power or dead driver
Check 24V/48V motor rail with a meter
DUMP_TMC STEPPER=stepper_x on a known-good channel to isolate
fixes: Correct uart_pin/address per board pinout
Restore VM before Klipper connect
Replace failed driver

errors.example: Unable to read tmc spi 'stepper_x' register GSTAT
errors.why: SPI transfer to a TMC5160/2130 returned an invalid pattern (all 0x00 or 0xFF) — MISO never toggled. Chip-select routing, SPI bus wiring, missing driver power, or a dead driver. All-zeros usually means no power/no chip; all-ones usually means MISO floating high (wrong CS, chip absent).
common_causes: cs_pin wrong or shared incorrectly across drivers
VM rail missing (5160 needs motor voltage for full register access on some rails)
SPI bus pins (spi_software_*/spi_bus) misconfigured
Driver killed by a prior short — s2g events often precede death
diagnostics: Compare all drivers on the same bus: one failing = CS/chip; all failing = bus/power
Measure VM at the driver
fixes: Fix cs_pin/bus config
Restore motor supply sequencing
Replace driver

errors.why: Driver die exceeded ~150°C and hardware-disabled outputs. The silicon protects itself; steps issued during the shutdown are lost (layer shift at best). Cause is thermal: too much RMS current for the cooling available, or chamber/enclosure heat soak.
common_causes: run_current set above the driver+heatsink thermal budget (Fracktal spec: 1.2A on TMC5160 X/Y)
Electronics-bay fan failed or airflow blocked
Heated-chamber machines soaking the electronics compartment
StealthChop at high speed forcing extra losses
diagnostics: otpw warnings in log before the error (visualize/grep 'otpw')
Check ELECTRONICS_CHAMBER_COOLING fan operation
IR-check driver heatsinks after a long print
fixes: Restore cooling; lower run_current 10–15%
spread_cycle for high-speed axes
Re-seat heatsinks with proper pads

errors.why: The driver's phase-A/B low-side detected current flow indicating that motor phase is shorted to ground — a hard electrical fault detection, latched until driver re-enable. Real short in the harness/motor, or a false trip from aggressive current spikes on marginal wiring.
common_causes: Pinched/abraded motor cable shorting to frame ground (check cable chains!)
Motor connector pin bridged by swarf/solder whisker
Motor winding insulation failure (measure phase-to-case resistance ≈ ∞)
Occasionally: dying driver false-reporting
diagnostics: Unplug motor at driver end → error persists on ENABLE = driver side; gone = harness/motor
Meter: phase-to-frame must be open; phase resistance ≈1–3Ω per coil
Inspect the full cable run, especially in drag chains
fixes: Repair harness/replace motor as isolated
Replace driver if it trips with no motor attached

errors.why: Phase output shorted to the motor supply rail (VM) — mirror image of short-to-ground: high-side detection tripped. Same physical fault class: harness or connector bridging a phase to VM, or driver failure.
common_causes: Harness short to a VM-carrying conductor
Connector bridge
Failed driver output stage
diagnostics: Same isolation procedure as short-to-ground
fixes: Repair the short; replace driver if it reports with motor disconnected

errors.raised_in: klippy/extras/tmc.py (DRV_STATUS ola/olb — reported, not always fatal)
errors.why: The driver couldn't push expected current through a phase — the circuit looks open. A genuinely broken wire/connector, OR a false positive: open-load detection is unreliable at very low currents, at standstill, and with stealthChop. Treat as real only if paired with a motor that's actually not moving/skipping.
common_causes: Broken crimp in motor connector (intermittent!)
Cable-chain conductor fatigue-break
False positive at low run_current/idle
diagnostics: Wiggle-test harness while running STEPPER_BUZZ
Measure phase continuity end-to-end (1–3Ω)
fixes: Re-crimp/replace harness section
Ignore if purely cosmetic at idle and motion is perfect

errors.why: The driver's internal charge pump (which generates gate-drive voltage for the output FETs) fell below its undervoltage threshold — VM (motor supply) is missing, sagging, or came up after driver init. Outputs are disabled until power is sane. On Fracktal machines this classically means the 24V rail dipped (PSU overload/brownout) or motor power sequencing.
common_causes: 24V/48V rail sag under simultaneous heater+motor load (weak/failing PSU)
Motor supply switched on AFTER Klipper initialized the driver
Loose VM terminal screw / crimp heating and dropping voltage
PSU overcurrent protection hiccuping
diagnostics: Scope/meter the VM rail during the failure window
pi_system_diagnostics + PSU checks in electronics_hardware_reference.json
Does it happen at heater-on moments? → PSU capacity
fixes: Fix supply sizing/wiring; torque terminal screws
Power sequencing: VM before/with logic
FIRMWARE_RESTART after restoring power re-inits drivers

errors.why: The driver reports it has been reset since configuration — its registers reverted to defaults while Klipper believed it was configured. VM glitched or ESD hit the chip; motion after an unnoticed reset would use wrong currents, hence the error.
common_causes: VM brownout blip
ESD/EMI event
Hot-unplugging a motor (inductive kick resets driver)
diagnostics: Correlate with power events; NEVER unplug motors while powered
fixes: Fix power stability; FIRMWARE_RESTART reconfigures drivers

patterns: Endstop x still triggered after retract
errors.example: Sensorless homing: 'Endstop x still triggered after retract' or homing stops instantly
errors.why: With sensorless homing the TMC's StallGuard load measurement is the endstop. If SGTHRS/sgt is too sensitive the 'stall' asserts immediately or never clears (instant/false trigger); too insensitive and the axis grinds the frame (no trigger). Homing speed changes StallGuard readings, so threshold is speed-specific.
common_causes: driver_SGTHRS mis-tuned
homing_speed changed after tuning
current too low for StallGuard signal
homing_retract_dist: 0 missing/wrong per docs
diagnostics: Tune per Klipper sensorless guide: find min/max working SGTHRS, use midpoint
Verify hold/run current during homing macro
fixes: Retune SGTHRS at the actual homing speed and current

errors.example: Heater extruder not heating at expected rate
errors.why: While driving the heater, temperature failed to rise by heating_gain (default 2°C) within check_gain_time (default 20s hotend/60s bed). The watchdog concluded the heater's power isn't reaching the sensor: dead/weak heater, detached thermistor (reads ambient while block heats — FIRE RISK this check exists to catch), sagging supply, or genuinely undersized heater vs airflow.
common_causes: Heater cartridge failed or connector burnt (measure: 40W@24V ≈ 14Ω; 60W ≈ 9.6Ω)
Thermistor no longer seated in the block (THE dangerous case)
Part-cooling fan blasting the block at high fan speeds
PSU rail sag when bed+hotend heat together
Silicone sock missing on a draft-exposed hotend
diagnostics: Physically verify thermistor and heater seated in the block FIRST
Measure heater resistance and supply voltage under load
visualize_data.py --type temperature — slope analysis: slow-but-steady = undersized; flat = dead
fixes: Re-seat/replace thermistor and heater; repair connectors
Only if hardware verified good and merely slow: raise check_gain_time (e.g. 35–60s) — never as a first response
PID_CALIBRATE after any thermal hardware change

errors.example: Heater extruder not maintaining target temperature (verify_heater max_error)
errors.why: While holding target, the integrated temperature shortfall exceeded max_error — the heater can't sustain the setpoint. Same physical causes as not-heating but during steady-state: airflow, drafts, failing heater, or PID badly tuned after hardware change.
common_causes: Fan-speed-correlated dips (cooling overwhelms heater)
Aging heater cartridge
PID tuned at different conditions
diagnostics: Temp graph vs fan speed events
PID_CALIBRATE at working conditions (fan 100% for hotend per docs guidance)
fixes: Sock/shroud fixes, new cartridge, retune PID

errors.why: A sensor read above configured max_temp → emergency shutdown. Either real overshoot (aggressive PID, stuck MOSFET) or sensor noise spike. A SHORTED MOSFET drives the heater at 100% regardless of firmware — Klipper can only cut power upstream if wired to; treat repeated occurrences as hardware-critical.
common_causes: PID overshoot after hardware change
Failed (shorted) heater MOSFET
Noise spike on thermistor line routed with motor wiring
diagnostics: Does temp keep rising AFTER shutdown? → MOSFET shorted, kill mains power
Overshoot only at target approach → PID
fixes: Replace board/MOSFET if shorted
PID_CALIBRATE
Reroute/shield thermistor wiring

motion_homing.description: Homing, probing, and kinematic limit errors — klippy/extras/homing.py, probe.py, toolhead.py

errors.example: Endstop x still triggered after retract
errors.why: After the homing move hit the endstop and retracted homing_retract_dist, the endstop STILL read triggered. Either the carriage physically didn't move away (mechanical bind, wrong retract distance), or the endstop signal is stuck: miswired (NC/NO), shorted, or electrically noisy.
common_causes: Endstop wire shorted / switch jammed
homing_retract_dist: 0 with a switch that needs travel to release
Sensorless: SGTHRS too sensitive (see TMC)
Crashed carriage bottomed out
diagnostics: QUERY_ENDSTOPS with carriage away from the switch — must read open
Manually click the switch while querying
fixes: Rewire/replace switch; set proper retract distance; retune sensorless

errors.example: No trigger on probe after full movement
errors.why: The homing/probing move completed its maximum travel without the trigger firing. The switch/probe is dead, unplugged, on the wrong pin, or the physical distance exceeds the configured travel (position_max/z range too small, probe above bed range).
common_causes: Probe unplugged/dead (BLTouch cable fatigue in cable chain)
Wrong pin or missing pull-up (^) in config
Z travel configured shorter than real distance
Inductive probe over non-metal bed area
diagnostics: QUERY_PROBE / QUERY_ENDSTOPS while manually triggering
Check connector at toolhead board (RP2040 CAN board on Fracktal)
fixes: Repair wiring/pin config; correct position_min/max; verify probe supply voltage

errors.why: At probe-start the probe already read triggered — Klipper refuses to probe because it can't detect the trigger transition. BLTouch pin still deployed/blocked, inverted signal logic, or nozzle already at/below trigger height.
common_causes: BLTouch pin fouled with plastic / bent
Signal inverted (missing '!' on pin)
Starting probe below trigger point
diagnostics: BLTOUCH_DEBUG COMMAND=pin_down/pin_up/self_test
QUERY_PROBE at safe Z height
fixes: Clean/replace BLTouch pin; fix pin inversion; raise Z before probing

errors.why: A commanded move targets coordinates outside the axis limits derived from position_min/position_max. G-code asked for space the machine doesn't have: slicer origin mismatch, missing/wrong bed size in slicer, offsets (KAMP/mesh, tool offsets on IDEX) pushing beyond limits, or homing set a wrong origin.
common_causes: Slicer bed size/origin ≠ printer.cfg limits
IDEX: T1 offset applied without dual-carriage range accounting (TwinDragon!)
Z hop at max Z
Mesh/adaptive purge outside bed
diagnostics: Which coordinate exceeded what limit (values in the message)
GET_POSITION after homing — sane origin?
fixes: Align slicer machine settings with printer.cfg
Fix dual_carriage offsets per klipper_IDEX conventions

errors.why: Motion was commanded while axes are in the unhomed state — Klipper has no reference. Not an error in hardware: a macro/print started without G28, or a prior error cleared homing state (any MCU shutdown unhomes).
common_causes: START_PRINT missing G28
Error earlier in session cleared homing
FORCE_MOVE expectations
diagnostics: Check macro chain for G28; check log for a prior shutdown that dropped homing
fixes: Home first; investigate why homing state was lost if unexpected

errors.why: An E-move was commanded while the extruder temperature is below min_extrude_temp — cold-extrusion protection. Either genuinely cold (heating skipped/failed) or the temperature never reached target before the macro extruded.
common_causes: Macro extrudes before M109 completes
Sensor under-reading (wrong sensor_type)
Testing without heating (use M302 consciously)
diagnostics: Check reported temp at the moment of the error
fixes: Fix macro sequencing; M302 P1 only for deliberate cold tests

errors.why: One G1's extrusion volume implies a bead cross-section larger than max_extrude_cross_section (default 4×nozzle²). Klipper computes E-distance × filament area ÷ XY-distance; a huge ratio means the G-code/config disagree about geometry: wrong rotation_distance (Fracktal TD-01 ≈4.72!), slicer in 'volumetric'/wrong filament diameter, or a legitimate purge blob line.
common_causes: rotation_distance wrong after extruder swap
filament_diameter mismatch (1.75 vs 2.85)
Slicer wipe/purge with tiny XY move
KAMP purge lines
diagnostics: Numbers in the message: how far off? 2× = config; 100× = units problem
Verify 100mm extrusion test measures 100mm
fixes: Calibrate rotation_distance
Fix slicer filament settings
Raise max_extrude_cross_section only for known purge macros

errors.example: Extrude only move too long (120.0mm vs 50.0mm)
errors.why: A pure E move (no XY) exceeded max_extrude_only_distance — guard against runaway load/unload commands. Filament-change macros routinely need more; raise the limit deliberately for those.
common_causes: LOAD/UNLOAD macros pushing bowden lengths
Slicer retract on filament change
diagnostics: Which macro issued it
fixes: Set max_extrude_only_distance to the real load path length (e.g. 200 for long PTFE runs — Fracktal PTFE lengths in PRINTER_VARIABLES)

canbus.description: CAN bus toolhead errors — Fracktal RP2040 toolhead ↔ Manta bridge at 500kbps (can2040 software CAN)

errors.raised_in: klippy/serialhdl.py CAN path (node discovery query unanswered)
errors.why: Klipper broadcast a discovery for the configured UUID and the node never answered. The toolhead board is unpowered, its firmware crashed/not flashed, the UUID is wrong (placeholder XXXXXXXXXXX from Fracktal template!), or the bus itself is down (interface, wiring, termination, bitrate).
common_causes: UUID placeholder never replaced after board swap (canbus_query.py to enumerate)
can0 interface down or wrong bitrate (must be 500000)
24V to toolhead board absent (harness connector)
Missing/double termination (need exactly two 120Ω, ~60Ω across CANH-CANL powered off)
Toolhead firmware needs reflash after Klipper update (Katapult/CAN bootloader)
diagnostics: ip -details link show can0 — UP? bitrate?
~/klippy-env/bin/python ~/klipper/scripts/canbus_query.py can0 (klipper service stopped)
Measure 60Ω CANH↔CANL (powered off); check toolhead power LED
fixes: Set real UUID; bring up can0 at 500k; fix power/termination; reflash toolhead via Katapult

errors.why: Established CAN comms then dropped: error frames accumulated until bus-off, or the node reset. Physical-layer noise (unshielded harness along steppers), marginal transceiver supply, or bus overload (ADXL345 streaming saturating 500kbps + motion traffic).
common_causes: Error counter growth → bus-off (check ip -s -d)
ADXL345 resonance testing while printing
Harness fatigue at drag chain
Ground offset between board grounds
diagnostics: ip -s -d link show can0 — error counters, bus-off count
candump can0 for error frames
Correlate drop with motion/probe events
fixes: Twisted-pair CANH/CANL, proper shield landing
Do resonance tests while idle
txqueuelen 1024 for can0

config_startup.description: Klippy configuration and startup errors — klippy/configfile.py, klippy.py

errors.example: Option 'sensor_type' in section 'extruder' must be specified
errors.why: printer.cfg parsing failed validation — a required option is missing, a section name is unknown (typo or module not in this Klipper version/fork), or an include pulled the wrong file set. Klipper stays in 'error' state until config is fixed + RESTART.
common_causes: Typo/missing option after manual edit
Fracktal: wrong PRINTER_*.cfg include combination (two printers uncommented, or toolhead1 on a single-extruder)
Module exists upstream but not in the installed fork (klipper_IDEX vs mainline)
diagnostics: Read the exact section/option in the message
firmware_analyzer.py --check all
remote_config_editor.py --validate before applying
fixes: Fix the named option; verify include hierarchy per Fracktal conventions (ONE printer, matching toolheads)

errors.example: SAVE_CONFIG section conflicts with included file
errors.why: The auto-generated SAVE_CONFIG block at the bottom of printer.cfg defines values (e.g. z_offset, PID, mesh) that also exist in an included file — Klipper refuses ambiguous duplicates. Common after moving configs between machines or editing included files directly (against Fracktal conventions).
common_causes: Manual edits duplicating saved values
Copied printer.cfg with stale SAVE_CONFIG block
diagnostics: Compare SAVE_CONFIG block against the named section
fixes: Delete the duplicated entry from ONE location (prefer keeping SAVE_CONFIG), RESTART

patterns: Once the underlying issue is corrected
errors.example: Once the underlying issue is corrected, use the 'FIRMWARE_RESTART' command
errors.why: Not itself an error — the banner shown AFTER any shutdown. The cause is the message directly above it in klippy.log. FIRMWARE_RESTART re-establishes MCU comms; RESTART alone won't clear MCU-level shutdowns.
common_causes: See the preceding error
diagnostics: klipper_log_parser.py — extract the shutdown reason above this banner
fixes: Fix root cause, then FIRMWARE_RESTART
